content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

June 8, 1995

Mr. Steven M. Little
2451 Calle Linares
Santa Barbara, California 93102

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-150

Dear Mr. Little:

You have requested advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ as they
pertain to your position as a Santa Barbara City Water
Commissioner.

QUESTION

May you participate in water rate decisions of the Santa
Barbara City Water Commission, since the decisions will affect
your residential and agricultural property interests?.

CONCLUSION

Although you have a conflict of interest with respect to the
agriculture irrigation water rate decision, you may participate in
the other water rate decisions provided the segmentation procedure
disclosed below, is followed.

FACTS

The Santa Barbara City Water Commission ("Commission") will
deliberate and vote upon water rates, including irrigation water
rates during May and June 1995. You and your wife own an avocado
grove and have a one-sixth share in an agricultural meter. You
receive water at an irrigation rate for agriculture. Your
investment in the avocado grove is in excess of $1000.

_______________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-95-150
Page 2


     City staff, at the direction of the city council,
commissioned a water rate study by an outside contractor, to
include consideration of all water rates, including rates for
business, residential and irrigation uses. There are three
categories of irrigation uses, which include parks and recreation,
agriculture and residential for large lots.

     The report indicates that there are 24,254 total accounts to
the municipal water system. There are 16,326 single family
dwelling accounts, 2,200 commercial accounts and 535 irrigation
accounts. Irrigation accounts include parks and recreation, large
lot residential and agricultural. There are 56 agriculture
metres and you share one meter with six other agriculture
interests.

     You have updated your request as of June 6, 1995, with the
following information. The commission received the contractor's
final report, which surveyed water rates, and is considering
alternatives to the recommendations contained in the final report.
The commission will probably make final recommendations in the
municipal and industrial water rates at the June 12, 1995,
meeting.

     The commission will not make final recommendations for the
residential irrigation and agriculture irrigation rates at the
June 12, 1995, meeting. It is expected that the commission will
ask the city council for further direction with regard to the
agriculture irrigation rates. The current options for this
category are either no change in the rate or a reduced rate with
certain land restrictions.

                              ANALYSIS

     The Act prohibits a public official from making, participat-
ing in, or using his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest. (Section 87100.) A public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his immediate family or on, among other
things:

          (a) Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

          (b) Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

          (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
     and other than loans by a commercial lending
     institution in the regular course of business on

File No. A-95-150
Page 3


terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dol-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

     (e)  Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

Section 87103(a)-(e).

     As a public official, you may not participate in a decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on any of your economic interests.
Your interests include your home, your avocado grove and your
investment in this business which exceeds $1,000.  (Section
87103(a) and (b).)

Foreseeability

     The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur.  To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere possibil-
ity; however, certainty is not required.  (Downey Cares v. Downey
Community Redevelopment Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-991;
Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed)).  The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest.  (Witt v. Morrow,
supra. at 823.)

     It is foreseeable that any changes in water rates may affect
the water rates for your residential property and your
agricultural business.  If the effect of any water rate decision
is material, you may not participate in the decision unless the
public generally exception applies.

Materiality

     Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's financial interest in a decision is
"material" as required by Section 87103.  If the official's
financial interest is directly involved in the decision, then
Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality.  If the
official's interest is indirectly involved, then the applicable
regulations are Regulations 18702.2 - 18702.6.

File No. A-95-150
Page 4


     The effect of decisions which "involve the imposition, repeal
or modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on
property, are considered material. (Regulation 18702.1 (a)(3)(C)),
copy enclosed.) Therefore, the water rate decisions will have a
material effect on both of your real property interests, which
include your residential property and your avocado grove.

Public Generally

     Even when the effect of a decision on a public official's
economic interests is material, participation is prohibited if
the effect of the decision on the public official's interest is
not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. For
the "public generally" exception to apply, a decision must affect
the official's interests in substantially the same manner as it
would affect a significant segment of the public. (Regulation
18703, copy enclosed.)

     Section (b)(3) of this regulation sets forth a special rule
for rates and assessments and states that:

     The financial effect of a governmental decision on an
official or the official's economic interest is
indistinguishable from the decision's effect on the
public generally if any of the following apply:

*****

     (3) The decision is made by the governing board of
a water, irrigation, or similar district to establish or
adjust assessments, taxes, fees, charges, or rates or
other similar decisions, such as the allocation of
services, which are applied on a proportional or
"across-the-board" basis on the official or the
official's economic interests and a significant segment
of the property owners or other persons receiving
services from the official's agency using the thresholds
set forth in subdivision (a)(1) above.

     Significant segment is defined in Regulation 18703(a)(1) as
follows:

*****

     (i) Ten percent or more of the population in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district
the official represents, or

     (ii) Ten percent or more of all property owners,
all home owners, or all households in the jurisdiction
of the official's agency or the district the official
represents, or

File No. A-95-150
Page 5


(iii) Fifty percent of all businesses in the
jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so
long as the segment is composed of persons other than a
single industry, trade, or profession; or,

It would appear that any decision regarding rate changes for
residential water users would affect your residential property in
a manner which is indistinguishable from the effect on a
significant segment of the public. However, since a significant
segment of the public does not have agricultural property or
agricultural meters, a decision regarding agriculture irrigation
water rates would not affect a significant segment of the public
in a way that it would affect your interests.

Therefore, you do not have a conflict of interest in the
decision regarding residential rates, but you do have a conflict
of interest in the decision regarding the agriculture irrigation
rates. You also do not have a conflict of interest in decisions
regarding the commercial water rates. For your ability to
participate in any of the decisions, please refer to the
discussion on segmentation, below.

You also inquired if Regulation 18703.3, which is a public
generally exception for members of appointed commissions, would
apply to you. One of the criteria of this regulation is that the
law that creates the commission requires that persons appointed to
the commission are appointed to represent a specific economic
interest. In Pike, the city Attorney, informed me that the
city charter provides no special representation qualifications for
the Board of Water Commissioners. Therefore, this regulation is
not applicable.

Segmentation

Although you have a conflict of interest with respect to one
of the decisions, it may be possible to proceed with the other
decisions regarding the water rates by segmenting the decisions.
(Schectman Advice Letter, No. A-87-159.) We have previously
advised the following procedure, which may be followed to permit
your participation:

(1) The decisions for which the councilmember has a disqualifying
financial interest should be segregated from the other
decisions.

(2) The decisions from which the councilmember is disqualified
should be considered first, and a final decision reached by
the city council without the councilmember participating in
any way.

(3) Once a decision has been made for which the councilmember has
a disqualifying financial interest, he may participate in the
deliberations regarding the other issues, so long as those

File No. A-95-150
Page 6


     deliberations do not result in a reopening or in any way
affect the decisions from which he was disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)

     Therefore, you may participate in the rate decisions for
which you do not have a conflict of interest only if the
segmentation procedure is followed and the agriculture irrigation
rate is considered first and a final decision is reached.

Exception

     Regulation 18700.1 (copy enclosed) provides that an otherwise
disqualified official may appear in the same manner as any other
member of the general public before his or her own agency in the
course of its prescribed governmental function to represent his or
her own personal interest in real property or a business entity,
which are wholly owned by the official or members of the
official's immediate family.

     Please note, this exception limits your involvement to your
own personal interests, and thus, you should clarify that you are
neither acting in any official capacity nor appearing before the
city council to represent any other person's interests.

     I trust this answers your question.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Jill Stecher
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak

cc:  Mr. Robert Pike
     City Attorney, Santa Barbara

Enclosures

Barbara City Water Commission,

agriculture irrigation water rate decision,
you may participate in
